sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Non-traumatic limb pain may be musculoskeletal, neuropathic, vascular, inflammatory, or referred. The goal is to identify whether the presentation is safe for conservative physio-led management or requires urgent medical escalation.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Limb pain without clear trauma
- Possible swelling, warmth, weakness, numbness, or altered function
- Symptoms worsened by load, posture, or repetition in some cases
- Pain may be diffuse or poorly localised

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Consider DVT, infection, acute limb ischaemia, compartment syndrome, inflammatory arthritis, fracture, or referred spinal pathology
- Sudden cold limb, severe swelling, disproportionate pain, fever, or rapidly progressive neurological deficit should escalate care
- Not every atraumatic presentation is safe for routine rehab advice

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen vascular, neurological, infectious, and weight-bearing red flags
- Use simple support and analgesia advice within scope if the limb appears stable
- Escalate promptly if swelling, colour change, fever, severe pain, or loss of function suggests more serious pathology

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Perform focused regional and spinal screen to distinguish local overload from referred or systemic causes
- Start activity modification and basic loading advice only once serious pathology is reasonably excluded
- Refer for imaging, medical review, or pathology workup when the pattern is atypical or persistent

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Document whether the patient can still safely use the limb for daily and occupational tasks
